request.question: Who wrote the fantasy novella which was reworked in 2014 with a series of paintings by a Spanish artist born in Olalla in 1954?

title: La Leocadia
The Wall of Serpents
The Castle of Iron
Gaspar de la Huerta
The Roaring Trumpet
The Ice Dragon
The Green Magician
Luis Royo
The Mathematics of Magic
Pilgrimage to the Fountain of San Isidro
sentences: La Leocadia (Spanish: Doña Leocadia) or The Seductress (Spanish: Una Manola) are names given to a mural by the Spanish artist Francisco Goya (1746–1828), completed sometime between 1819–23, as one of his series of 14 "Black Paintings".
It shows a woman commonly identified as Goya's maid, companion and, most likely lover, Leocadia Weiss.
She is dressed in a dark, almost funeral maja dress, and leans against what is either a mantelpiece or burial mound, as she looks outward at the viewer with a sorrowful expression.

"Leocadia" is one of the final of the "Black Paintings", which he painted in his seventies at a time when he was consumed by political, physical and psychological turmoil, after he fled to the country from his position as court painter in Madrid.
The Wall of Serpents is a fantasy novella by American science fiction and fantasy authors L. Sprague de Camp and Fletcher Pratt.
The fourth story in their Harold Shea series, it was first published in the June 1953 issue of the fantasy pulp magazine "Fantasy Fiction".

It first appeared in book form, together with its sequel, "The Green Magician", in the collection "Wall of Serpents", issued in hardcover by Avalon Books in 1960; the book has been reissued by a number of other publishers since.
It has also been reprinted in various anthologies and collections, including "Great Short Novels of Adult Fantasy I" (1972), "The Complete Compleat Enchanter" (1989), and "" (2007).
It has been translated into Italian and German.
The Castle of Iron is the title of a fantasy novella by American authors L. Sprague de Camp and Fletcher Pratt, and of the novel into which it was later expanded by the same authors.
It was the third story (and afterwards the second volume) in their Harold Shea series.
As a 35,000 word novella it was first published in the fantasy magazine "Unknown" for April, 1941.
The revised and expanded novel version was first published in hardcover by Gnome Press in 1950, and in paperback by Pyramid Books in 1962.
The book has been reprinted by a number of other publishers since its first appearance.
An E-book edition was published by Gollancz's SF Gateway imprint on September 29, 2011 as part of a general release of de Camp's works in electronic form.
The novel has been combined with other books in the series in the omnibus editions "The Compleat Enchanter" (1975), "The Complete Compleat Enchanter" (1989) and "" (2007).
It has also been translated into Italian.
Gaspar de la Huerta (1645–1714) was a Spanish artist born at Campillo de Altobuey in Cuenca.

At an early age, seeking instruction in Valencia, he fell into the hands of Jesualda Sanchez, the bustling widow of Pedro Infant, a third-rate painter, who continued on her own account her husband's school for the manufacture of religious pictures.
La Huerta, nevertheless, attained some skill as a draughtsman and colourist, and married the widow's well-dowered daughter.
Working for moderate prices he found abundant employment in the neighbouring churches and convents.
For the Franciscans he painted the "Jubilee of the Porciuncula", and for the Dominicans the picture which long served as a veil to the wondrous image of "Our Lady of the Forsaken".
He died at Valencia in 1714.
The Museum of Valencia possesses a pleasing picture of "Christ and the Virgin enthroned", the latter with the bright complexion peculiar to Valencian beauty.
"The Roaring Trumpet" is a fantasy novella by American writers L. Sprague de Camp and Fletcher Pratt.
The initial story in their Harold Shea series, it was first published in the May 1940 issue of the fantasy pulp magazine "Unknown".

It first appeared in book form, together with its sequel, "The Mathematics of Magic", in the collection "The Incomplete Enchanter", issued in hardcover by Henry Holt and Company in 1941, and in paperback by Pyramid Books in 1960.

It has since been reprinted in various collections by numerous other publishers, including "The Compleat Enchanter" (1975), "The Incompleat Enchanter" (1979), "The Complete Compleat Enchanter" (1989), and "" (2007).
It has been translated into Dutch and Italian.
In 2016, the story was shortlisted for the Retro Hugo Award for Best Novella.
The Ice Dragon is a children's fantasy novella by George R. R. Martin, originally published in 1980 in the Ace Books anthology "Dragons of Light".
It was later included in Martin's 1987 collection "Portraits of His Children".
The story was reworked in 2007 with artwork by Yvonne Gilbert and again in 2014 with a series of original paintings by Luis Royo.
The Green Magician is a fantasy novella by American writers L. Sprague de Camp and Fletcher Pratt.
The fifth story in their Harold Shea series, it was first published in the November 1954 issue of the fantasy pulp magazine "Beyond Fiction".

It first appeared in book form, together with "The Wall of Serpents", in the collection "Wall of Serpents", issued in hardcover by Avalon Books in 1960; the book has been reissued by a number of other publishers since.

It has also been reprinted in various magazines, anthologies and collections, including "The Dragon" (June–July 1978), "The Complete Compleat Enchanter" (1989), "Masterpieces of Fantasy and Enchantment" (1988), and "" (2007).
It has been translated into Italian and German.
Luis Royo (born 1954) is a Spanish artist born in Olalla, a village in the Aragonese province of Teruel.

He is best known for his fantasy illustrations published in numerous art books, magazines such as "Heavy Metal" and various other media including book and music CD covers, video games and Tarot cards.
"The Mathematics of Magic" is a fantasy novella by American writers L. Sprague de Camp and Fletcher Pratt, the second story in their Harold Shea series.
It was first published in the August 1940 issue of the fantasy pulp magazine "Unknown".

It first appeared in book form, together with the preceding novella, "The Roaring Trumpet", in the collection "The Incomplete Enchanter", issued in hardcover by Henry Holt and Company in 1941, and in paperback by Pyramid Books in 1960.

It has since been reprinted in various collections by numerous other publishers, including "The Compleat Enchanter" (1975), "The Incompleat Enchanter" (1979), "The Complete Compleat Enchanter" (1989), and "" (2007).
It has been translated into Dutch and Italian.
In 2016, the story was shortlisted for the Retro Hugo Award for Best Novella.

Pilgrimage to the Fountain of San Isidro or The Holy Office (Spanish: Peregrinación a la fuente de San Isidro or El Santo Official ) are names given to an oil mural by the Spanish artist Francisco Goya (1746–1828), probably completed between 1821 and 1823.
The mural is one of the fourteen Black Paintings that Goya applied in oil on the plaster walls of his house.
Between 1874 and 1878 the paintings were transferred to canvas supports under the direction of the art restorer of the Museo del Prado, Salvador Martinez Cubells.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Task: Compress the grounded first-hop evidence into a single concise, retrieval-oriented intermediate state to guide the next-hop retrieval. Do not produce the final answer or perform any new retrieval here.

Required content of `summary` (single field):
- A short header identifying the primary topic(s) found in the passages (1 line).
- A concise enumeration of the concrete facts supported verbatim by the provided passages: list entity mentions and directly supported factual spans (dates, years, titles, roles). Use very short quoted fragments (<= ~15 words) only when those words appear verbatim in the passages. Keep to facts present in the passages; do not add or infer new facts.
- Explicitly expose any ambiguity or missing information that blocks answering: if multiple candidate entities or page variants appear, enumerate those candidates and label the name as ambiguous; if a required target fact is missing or ambiguous, label it as the missing fact and identify the bridge entity that connects this hop to the next.
- Prioritized next-hop retrieval directives (1–4 items): concrete page titles or disambiguation pages to fetch, explicit article sections to inspect (e.g., lede/infobox, "Career", "Publication", "History"), or tight search keywords. Order them by priority and be specific (include possible disambiguation terms or section names).

Formatting and style constraints:
- Keep the entire `summary` concise: a short header plus 1–4 brief sentences or 2–6 short bullet lines in total.
- Preserve the habit of extracting and citing concrete facts verbatim from the passages (names, dates, passage labels).
- Do not output a final answer, comparison, or any numeric fact that purports to resolve the question here. Even if the passages fully and unambiguously contain all required facts, present that evidence and explicitly state "no additional retrieval needed," but defer producing any final answer to a downstream component.
- If a name or term has multiple distinct variants in the passages, do not pick one without disambiguating evidence; enumerate candidates instead.
- Do not invent or add facts not present in the supplied passages. Avoid long verbatim quotations; prefer short extracts (<= ~15 words) or paraphrase.

Output expectations: produce exactly one field `summary` containing the concise, retrieval-oriented summary that meets the above requirements.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Produce exactly one compact, retrieval-focused second-hop query in the field `query`. Use only the provided `question` and `summary_1` as inputs. Do not include any explanation, answer, fallback text, lists, or additional fields — the output must be a single one-line query string.

Decision templates (choose the single template that matches the missing evidence implied by summary_1):
- Connector between two named items: emit a short interrogative naming both items and requesting the linking evidence (e.g., artist album recording) in one line. Retain the visible item tokens from the inputs.
- Metadata for a single anchor title: emit a compact keyword-style query consisting of the anchor plus brief attribute keywords (for example: artist genre).
- Temporal or aggregate fact: include the year or season token plus the entity type and a short cue such as season count.

Strict formatting rules:
- Emit exactly one line and exactly one string in `query` containing only the query text.
- Keep the query compact (aim for ≤ 10–12 words/tokens) and free of punctuation or explanatory clauses.
- Preserve visible tokens from `question` and `summary_1` (title words, years, or entity names) to anchor retrieval.
- Do not answer, conclude, enumerate candidates, assert hidden facts, or include user-facing instructions.

Produce the `query` field now.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Instructions (must follow exactly):
- Emit a single field named `summary` containing only 1–3 concise sentences that integrate the first-hop `context` and the second-hop `passages` into a grounded post-retrieval state.
- If the `context` already contains a concise answer sentence, keep that sentence as the first sentence of `summary` unless the `passages` clearly contradict it; append supporting sentence(s) after it rather than replacing it.
- Preserve the primary answer name(s) exactly as they appear in the inputs (context or passages) unless those names clearly conflict with stronger passage content.
- When one or more `passages` supply short biographical, membership, partnership, or corroborating facts about a named entity from the `context`, append at most one brief supporting sentence per relevant named person. Each such supporting sentence should:
  - begin with the entity's canonical name as shown in the passages (if available),
  - paraphrase up to one or two key attributes present in the passages (examples: nationality, award/winner status, current affiliation/team, partner identity, founding/formation fact, birth date), and
  - avoid long verbatim copying (prefer paraphrase; use short quotes only when a brief exact phrase from a passage is necessary for clarity).
- If the question requires naming both members of a partnership or collaborator pair, name both people in the summary and, if passages provide partner-specific attributes, include one brief attribute for each as separate short clauses or sentences if needed.
- Do not invent facts not present in the provided passages. If passages conflict on a specific attribute, do not synthesize a new reconciled fact; instead prefer the passage that most directly addresses the question or omit the disputed attribute (or state necessary ambiguity only when needed to avoid a false claim).
- Keep the `summary` compact and focused: avoid extraneous biography, discography/chart data, or unrelated commentary. When `context` is absent or is not a concise answer, produce a 1–2 sentence integrated summary naming the relevant entity(ies) and including at most one brief paraphrased attribute drawn from the passages.

Output requirement: exactly one JSON field `summary` whose value is the integrated 1–3 sentence summary.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Instructions:
- Use only `summary_1` and `summary_2` as the evidential basis for the final decision; do not add new facts or external information.
- First determine whether the question is a binary (yes/no) proposition and whether both `summary_1` and `summary_2` clearly and unambiguously support the same binary outcome.
- If BOTH summaries unambiguously assert the same binary conclusion, emit exactly one lowercase token as the entire content of `answer`: use "yes" for an affirmative conclusion and "no" for a negative conclusion. The `answer` field must contain that single token and nothing else (no punctuation, no extra whitespace, no explanation).
- Prefer `summary_2` as the decisive statement when it clarifies or restates `summary_1`, but still require that both summaries support the same binary judgment before emitting the single-token `answer`.
- If the summaries conflict, if either summary hedges, is ambiguous, silent about the binary relation, or otherwise fails to clearly agree, do NOT apply the single-token rule; instead produce the module's normal fallback/unresolved output for `answer`.
- Always remain truthful to the visible summaries and do not alter their factual conclusions.

response.hop1_query: Who wrote the fantasy novella which was reworked in 2014 with a series of paintings by a Spanish artist born in Olalla in 1954?
hop1_docs: The Ice Dragon | The Ice Dragon is a children's fantasy novella by George R. R. Martin, originally published in 1980 in the Ace Books anthology "Dragons of Light".  It was later included in Martin's 1987 collection "Portraits of His Children".  The story was reworked in 2007 with artwork by Yvonne Gilbert and again in 2014 with a series of original paintings by Luis Royo.

Luis Royo | Luis Royo (born 1954) is a Spanish artist born in Olalla, a village in the Aragonese province of Teruel.  He is best known for his fantasy illustrations published in numerous art books, magazines such as "Heavy Metal" and various other media including book and music CD covers, video games and Tarot cards.

Les Femmes d'Alger | Les Femmes d'Alger (Women of Algiers) is a series of 15 paintings and numerous drawings by the Spanish cubist artist Pablo Picasso.  The series, created in 1954-1955, was inspired by Eugène Delacroix's 1834 painting "The Women of Algiers in their Apartment" (French: "Femmes d'Alger dans leur appartement" ).  The series is one of several painted by Picasso in tribute to artists that he admired.
Jesús Olalla | Jesús "Josu" Olalla Iraeta (born July 15, 1971) is a Spanish handball player who competed in the 1996 Summer Olympics and in the 2000 Summer Olympics.

Chris Martin (artist) | Chris Martin (born 1954) is an American artist based in Brooklyn, New York.  Martin was born in Washington DC.  He makes abstract paintings with elements of collage which reference New Age and nature imagery.  Martin is influenced by artists such as Paul Feeley, Yayoi Kusama, Alfred Jensen and others who are also concerned with the formal qualities of painting.

Sarah Dollard | Sarah Dollard (born 21 January 1980) is an Australian screenwriter, living and working in the United Kingdom.  She made her start in writing for television on the long-running Australian soap opera "Neighbours", before moving to the UK in 2008.  Stints working in the script departments of British fantasy and science fiction TV shows, "Merlin" and "Primeval", led to writing episodes of BBC Three supernatural television series "Being Human" and British spy thriller "The Game", which first broadcast on BBC America.  She created and wrote the Welsh romantic comedy series "Cara Fi" (Love Me), which debuted on S4C in 2014.  She wrote the episode "Face the Raven" for Series 9 and the episode "Thin Ice" for Series 10 of Doctor Who.

The Copper Promise | The Copper Promise is a 2013 fantasy novella series by Jen Williams and her debut work.  It was initially released between December 2013 and January 2014 as a set of four e-book novellas and has since been published in paperback format as a single volume through Headline (ISBN  ).
response.summary_1:
Primary topics: "The Ice Dragon" (novella) and Luis Royo (Spanish artist, born in Olalla, 1954).

- From passage [1]: '"The Ice Dragon" is a "children's fantasy novella" by George R. R. Martin'; "originally published in 1980"; "reworked in 2014 with a series of original paintings by Luis Royo."
- From passage [2]: 'Luis Royo (born 1954) is a Spanish artist born in Olalla.'
- Ambiguity/missing facts: None in these passages — the novella, its 2014 reworking, the artist, and the artist's birth year/place are explicitly stated. No additional retrieval needed to identify the author based on provided passages.
- Next-hop retrievals (priority):
  1. "The Ice Dragon" — inspect lede/infobox and "Publication history" or "Editions" sections for 2014 edition details and illustration credits.
  2. "George R. R. Martin" — check "Works" / "Short fiction" sections to confirm authorship and publication listings.
  3. "Luis Royo" — inspect lede and "Works" or "Collaborations" for confirmation of 2014 paintings associated with the novella.
  4. Search keywords if needed: The Ice Dragon 2014 Luis Royo edition; "The Ice Dragon" reworked 2014 paintings.
hop2_docs: Dragons of Ice | Dragons of Ice is the start of the second major story arc in the "Dungeons & Dragons" Dragonlance series of game modules.  It is one of the 14 DL modules published by TSR between 1984 and 1986.  Its cover features a painting of a white dragon attacking sail powered ice boats by Larry Elmore.  The module launches players into the story of the second book of the Dragonlance Chronicles, "Dragons of Winter Night".

A Dance with Dragons | A Dance with Dragons is the fifth of seven planned novels in the epic fantasy series "A Song of Ice and Fire" by American author George R. R. Martin.  In some areas the paperback edition was published in two parts titled "Dreams and Dust" and "After the Feast".  It was the first novel in the series to be published following the commencement of the HBO series adaptation, "Game of Thrones", and runs to 1,040 pages.

London Dragons | The University of London Dragons, formerly known as the University of London Union Purples, are a British student ice hockey team based in London, England.  They compete in Division One of the British Universities Ice Hockey Association.  They are the most successful student ice hockey team in the history of the BUIHA.  Dragons A has won the Division 1 playoff final (or SuperFinal) in 2008, 2011, 2013, 2015 and 2017 and the Tier 1 Nationals tournament on 2003, 2005, 2008 and 2012.
Fire and Ice: The Dragon Chronicles | Fire & Ice: The Dragon Chronicles is a 2008 action film by MediaPro Pictures and the Sci Fi Channel directed by Jean-Christophe Comar.

Dragons: Fire and Ice | Dragons: Fire & Ice is a 2004 computer-animated CGI adventure film and the first of a two-part series based on the Mega Bloks toyline.  The film was released directly to DVD and is the story of two unlikely heroes, Prince Dev of the Norvagen and Princess Kyra of the Draigar.  These two band together along with their dragons, Targon and Aurora, to help save Dragon World and their world from evil.  A sequel, "", was released in 2005.

The Ice Dragon | The Ice Dragon is a children's fantasy novella by George R. R. Martin, originally published in 1980 in the Ace Books anthology "Dragons of Light".  It was later included in Martin's 1987 collection "Portraits of His Children".  The story was reworked in 2007 with artwork by Yvonne Gilbert and again in 2014 with a series of original paintings by Luis Royo.

China Dragon | The China Dragon () was a professional ice hockey team which played its home games in three different cities in China, including Harbin, Qiqihar, and Shanghai.  The team was a member of Asia League Ice Hockey between 2007 and 2017.  The team was formed in 1954 as two different clubs, Harbin and Qiqihar, as part of the China League.  In 2004, the clubs joined Asia League Ice Hockey and in 2007 they merged into a single club, China Sharks, backed by the National Hockey League's San Jose Sharks for financial reasons.  The team was renamed the China Dragon in 2009 when San Jose pulled out.  The team was dissolved following the 2016–17 Asia League Ice Hockey season.
hop2_titles: Fire and Ice: The Dragon Chronicles
response.summary_2: The Ice Dragon is a children's fantasy novella by George R. R. Martin. Luis Royo is a Spanish artist born in Olalla in 1954 who produced a series of original paintings for a 2014 reworking of the novella.
